iucnNote: Heavily targeted across the Indo-Pacific; regional stocks assessed as Endangered. Choose farmed or smaller portions where possible.
sizeWeight: 300 g – 4 kg (sold whole or in steaks)
habitat: Shallow coastal and estuarine waters with muddy bottoms
flavour: Sweet, clean and delicate with almost no fishiness
texture: Soft, fine flakes that stay moist; very tender
preparation: Best steamed whole or as a thick cutlet. Pat dry, score the thickest part, and steam just until the flesh turns opaque. Excellent for babies and the elderly because of the soft flesh.
description: Silvery elongated body with a slightly pointed snout and several thread-like filaments trailing from the lower part of the pectoral fin — the namesake 'threads'. Often sold as thick boneless steaks.

iucnNote: Listed as Vulnerable due to intense fishing pressure across its range. A festive favourite, so demand is high — buy sparingly.
habitat: Coastal waters over muddy substrates, often in schools
texture: Fine, soft and almost boneless flesh
preparation: The classic Teochew treatment: steam whole with salted vegetables, tomato and sour plum. Score both sides lightly so the heat penetrates evenly. Avoid overcooking — the flesh dries out quickly.
description: Distinctly flat, diamond-shaped silvery body with no pelvic fins and a deeply forked tail. The whole fish looks almost circular when viewed side-on.

iucnNote: Near Threatened — a long-lived predator vulnerable to overfishing. Usually sold pre-cut into steaks.
habitat: Open coastal waters, a fast-swimming pelagic predator
texture: Firm, meaty and dense — holds its shape well
preparation: The default fish for sliced fish soup and fish curry. Cut across the grain into thick steaks. Sear or pan-fry for soup, or simmer gently in curry. Its firmness makes it forgiving for beginners.
description: Long torpedo-shaped silvery body with faint wavy vertical bars and small finlets running toward the tail. Almost always displayed as cross-section steaks with a central bone.

iucnNote: Least Concern and widely farmed in Singapore and the region — one of the more sustainable everyday choices.
preparation: Versatile: steam whole with soy and ginger, or fillet for pan-frying. Keep the skin on for crisping. A reliable all-rounder that forgives slight overcooking.
description: Streamlined silvery-grey body with a distinctly concave forehead, large mouth and forked tail. Often sold live in tanks or whole on ice.

iucnNote: Least Concern, though prized and pricey. Increasingly farmed.
habitat: Coral reefs, rocky areas and coastal mangroves
flavour: Sweet and refined, considered a premium steaming fish
preparation: A premium steaming fish — Cantonese style with light soy, ginger and scallion lets its sweetness shine. Larger fish can be cut into thick steaks for curry.
description: Coppery-gold to bronze body, sometimes with a dark spot on each side near the lateral line, with a typical snapper profile: sloping head and a single continuous dorsal fin.

iucnNote: Least Concern and commonly farmed in cages around the region.
habitat: Mangroves and estuaries when young, reefs as adults
preparation: The everyday 'red fish' for assam (tamarind) curry and fish head curry. Robust enough to simmer without falling apart. Also good steamed or fried.
description: Reddish to coppery body, often darker on the back, with a classic snapper shape. The go-to inexpensive red fish in wet markets, frequently sold as a head and collar for curry.

iucnNote: Near Threatened in the wild; much of the market supply is now farmed. Prefer farmed grouper.
flavour: Sweet and delicate with a hint of richness
texture: Firm, gelatinous and moist — prized for its silky skin
preparation: A banquet steaming fish. Steam whole with superior soy, ginger and spring onion. The skin and head are gelatinous and treasured. Larger ones make a luxurious fish head curry.
description: Stout, robust body in olive-brown to grey, covered in small orange or brown spots, with a large mouth and rounded tail. Often sold live in tanks.

iucnNote: Least Concern — an abundant, fast-breeding schooling fish and a sustainable budget pick.
texture: Soft, fine flesh with small bones
preparation: Almost always deep-fried whole until crisp — bones and all — and eaten with sambal or chilli. Marinate with turmeric and salt first. A nasi lemak staple.
description: Small slender silvery fish with a single bright yellow stripe running from the eye to the tail. Sold in heaps by the kilo.

iucnNote: Data Deficient — not formally assessed, but a fast-growing, abundant schooling fish generally regarded as a sound budget choice.
flavour: Rich, oily and full-flavoured (high in omega-3)
preparation: Grill or fry whole, or simmer in assam pedas (sour-spicy tamarind gravy). Score the sides and rub with turmeric and salt before cooking to tame the oiliness.
description: Compact silvery-blue mackerel with a rounded body, large eyes and a faint golden band along the flank. Smaller and deeper-bodied than the batang.

iucnNote: Least Concern and almost entirely farmed — an affordable, low-impact freshwater fish.
flavour: Very mild, slightly sweet; can be earthy if pond-raised
preparation: Best deep-fried whole and finished with sweet-sour sauce, or steamed with plenty of ginger and garlic to lift any muddiness. Score deeply so seasoning penetrates.
description: Deep-bodied freshwater fish, grey to reddish (red tilapia is common), with a long spiny dorsal fin and vertical barring. Usually sold live.

iucnNote: Least Concern and extensively farmed across Southeast Asia.
flavour: Mild and slightly sweet, milky as the name suggests
texture: Soft and tender but notoriously bony
preparation: Famous for its many fine bones. Deep-fry until crisp so smaller bones soften, or buy deboned belly. Popular fried and served with sambal, or in soups.
description: Streamlined silvery fish with a large forked tail, small toothless mouth and big eyes covered by a fatty eyelid. Looks sleek and bright silver.

iucnNote: Many wild stingray and ray species are Vulnerable or Endangered due to slow breeding and heavy fishing. Eat occasionally and check the species.
habitat: Sandy and muddy seabeds in coastal waters
flavour: Mild and slightly sweet, soaks up marinades beautifully
texture: Soft, stringy flesh that separates into fibres along the cartilage
preparation: The hawker classic: slather a wing with sambal, wrap in banana leaf and grill (BBQ sambal stingray). The cartilage stays soft and the flesh peels off in strands.
description: Sold as flat, triangular 'wings' of pinkish flesh with visible cartilage ridges fanning out like a hand. The whole ray is a flat diamond shape with a long thin tail.

iucnNote: Least Concern; the most widely farmed prawn in the region. Farmed sourcing varies in sustainability.
habitat: Coastal waters and brackish aquaculture ponds
flavour: Sweet and rich with a deep prawn aroma
preparation: The premium all-purpose prawn. Leave the shell on for grilling, cereal prawns or salted-egg prawns — the shell carries flavour. Devein along the back. Cook just until they curl into a C and turn coral.
description: Large prawn with bold dark grey-and-cream banding across the shell (the 'tiger' stripes), turning bright orange-red when cooked. Long antennae and a prominent rostrum.

iucnNote: Not formally evaluated by the IUCN. A major wild and farmed food prawn across Southeast Asia.
habitat: Estuaries and coastal waters with muddy bottoms
flavour: Clean and sweet, slightly milder than tiger prawns
preparation: The everyday cooking prawn for noodles, sambal and stir-fries. Wonderful in white pepper or cereal prawns. Keep heads on for stock — they hold lots of flavour.
description: Pale yellow-to-translucent prawn with faintly banana-coloured legs and antennae, and a smooth unbanded shell. Slimmer than the tiger prawn.

iucnNote: Least Concern; widely caught and farmed. The big crab behind chilli and black pepper crab.
flavour: Sweet, rich meat with a luxurious roe in females
preparation: The icon of Singapore seafood. Buy live and lively (tie the claws). Chop into sections and crack the claws before stir-frying in chilli or black pepper sauce. Females (rounder apron) carry prized orange roe.
description: Large heavy crab with a smooth olive-green to brown shell, enormous powerful claws and the last pair of legs flattened into paddles. Usually sold live with claws bound.

iucnNote: Data Deficient — heavily fished but not formally assessed. A popular, lower-cost crab.
habitat: Sandy and seagrass areas in shallow coastal waters
texture: Lighter and flakier than mud crab; less meat per gram
preparation: Sweetest steamed simply or stir-fried with salted egg or ginger and spring onion. Males (blue-tinged, blue claws) have firmer meat; females carry roe. Best cooked the day you buy it.
description: Lighter crab with long swimming-paddle hind legs and a beautifully mottled blue-and-white (males) or mottled brown (females) shell with spiny edges and long slender claws.

iucnNote: Not formally evaluated by the IUCN. A by-catch delicacy increasingly popular in zi char.
habitat: Burrows in sandy and muddy seabeds
flavour: Very sweet, somewhere between prawn and lobster
preparation: Deep-fry then toss with garlic and chilli, or salted egg. Snip down both sides of the underside shell with scissors to free the meat. Handle with care — the front limbs are sharp.
description: Long flattened body resembling a cross between a prawn and a mantis, with large folding raptorial front claws and a segmented, armoured tail fan. Pale grey with faint banding.

iucnNote: Data Deficient — fast-growing and short-lived, generally considered resilient, but not formally assessed.
texture: Springy and tender if cooked fast, rubbery if overcooked
preparation: Clean by pulling out the head and the clear plastic-like quill. Score the inside of the body in a crosshatch so it curls. Cook hard and fast (under 2 minutes) or braise long and slow — anything in between turns it rubbery.
description: Torpedo-shaped translucent-to-pinkish body with long fins running almost the full length of the mantle, two long tentacles and eight arms. Sold whole and glossy.

iucnNote: Data Deficient — a major fishery species across the region that has not been formally assessed.
flavour: Sweet and more deeply savoury than squid
texture: Thicker and meatier than squid; pleasantly chewy
preparation: Remove the hard internal cuttlebone and ink sac (keep the ink for black sauces). Its thick mantle is great scored and stir-fried with sambal or kang kong, or blanched for Teochew-style cold cuttlefish.
description: Broad, flattened oval body, thicker than a squid, with a fringing fin around the entire mantle and a hard internal cuttlebone. Often has a zebra-like banding when fresh.

iucnNote: Least Concern; short-lived and fast-growing. Less common in wet markets than squid.
flavour: Sweet and mineral, deeper than squid
preparation: Tenderise by simmering gently for 30–45 minutes (a wine cork in the pot is the old trick) before grilling or stir-frying. Rinse the suckers well. Slice thin for salads.
description: Soft bulbous head (mantle) with eight long arms lined with two rows of suckers, and no internal shell. Mottled reddish-brown, able to change colour.

iucnNote: Not formally evaluated by the IUCN; extensively farmed on mudflats across the region.
habitat: Intertidal mudflats; farmed on tidal beds
flavour: Rich, irony and intensely 'of the sea' (the red 'blood' juice)
preparation: The char kway teow and laksa essential. Blanch very briefly in just-boiled water (10–20 seconds) so they stay plump and juicy — overcooking makes them tough and rubbery. Always source from clean, reputable suppliers and cook thoroughly enough to be safe.
description: Small, heart-shaped shells with deep ribbed grooves radiating across the surface, off-white to grey. Named for the reddish fluid (haemoglobin) inside.

iucnNote: Least Concern; widely farmed on ropes and a low-impact, sustainable protein.
habitat: Farmed on ropes and posts in coastal waters
preparation: Scrub the shells and pull off the wiry 'beard'. Discard any that stay open when tapped raw, or stay closed after cooking. Steam with garlic, chilli and Thai basil, or simmer in curry. Cooks in 4–5 minutes.
description: Elongated teardrop shell with a vivid emerald-green margin fading to brown, and a smooth, glossy surface. Often sold live in mesh bags.

iucnNote: Not formally evaluated; farmed oysters are filter feeders and among the most sustainable seafood.
sizeWeight: Sold whole or shucked by weight
habitat: Farmed on coastal beds and racks
preparation: Small shucked oysters are the heart of orh luak (oyster omelette). Rinse gently to remove grit. For omelettes, fold in near the end so they stay juicy. Larger ones can be steamed with garlic or eaten raw if sashimi-grade and very fresh.
description: Rough, craggy grey shells with an irregular teardrop shape; the meat is plump, beige and glistening. Often sold pre-shucked in tubs for cooking.

iucnNote: Not formally evaluated; widely farmed and a quick, sustainable weeknight seafood.
habitat: Sandy and muddy intertidal flats; farmed
preparation: Soak in salted water 1–2 hours so they spit out sand. The zi char classic: stir-fry hard and fast with sliced chilli, garlic and a splash of wine until they just open. Discard any that don't open.
description: Small oval shells with fine concentric ridges and pretty mottled tan, grey and brown patterns. Sold live and tightly closed (or snapping shut when handled).

iucnNote: Not formally evaluated; hand-gathered or farmed. A prized restaurant-style shellfish.
flavour: Sweet, rich and a little more intense than clams
preparation: Soak to purge sand. Steam quickly with minced garlic, glass noodles and light soy, or with garlic and Chinese wine — they cook in 3–4 minutes and toughen fast. Trim off the dark digestive tip before eating.
description: Long, narrow tube-shaped shells like an old straight razor or a stick of bamboo, golden-brown and slightly curved, with the meat protruding from both ends.

iucnNote: Reassessed as Near Threatened in 2023 as wild stocks decline. The fish you buy is almost always farmed (Norway, Tasmania, Scotland) — check for responsibly-farmed sourcing.
habitat: Cold North Atlantic waters; farmed in sea cages
texture: Soft, large moist flakes; high oil content
preparation: Imported but hugely popular for salmon fish soup and pan-searing. Keep the skin on and crisp it in a hot dry pan. Don't overcook — pull it while the centre is just translucent. Also superb raw if sashimi-grade.
description: Sold as bright orange-pink fillets and steaks with pale fat lines and silver skin. Unmistakable colour versus local white fish.

iucnNote: Globally Least Concern after stocks recovered (2021), but the Indian Ocean population is overfished and assessed Vulnerable. Check the source region.
preparation: Best seared rare or eaten raw (poke, sashimi) when very fresh, since the lean flesh dries out if cooked through. Can also be cubed into a curry. Slice across the grain.
description: Sold as deep red, dense boneless loins or steaks with a fine grain. Whole fish are torpedo-shaped with long yellow finlets.

iucnNote: Least Concern; a hardy, widespread freshwater fish, often farmed.
habitat: Freshwater ponds, drains, swamps and rivers
texture: Firm, gelatinous flesh that flakes cleanly with few bones
preparation: Traditionally eaten after surgery or childbirth for its 'wound-healing' reputation. The classic is a clear sliced fish soup with ginger; also steamed or fried. Skin is rich in collagen.
description: Long cylindrical body with a flattened, snake-like head and mottled dark-grey to brown markings, with a long continuous dorsal fin. Usually sold live.

iucnNote: Least Concern; a premium freshwater fish, increasingly farmed to meet restaurant demand.
habitat: Slow freshwater rivers, ponds and reservoirs
flavour: Exceptionally sweet and delicate — a banquet fish
texture: Firm, silky white flesh that lifts off the bone in clean fillets
preparation: A prized steaming fish, treated Cantonese-style with light soy, ginger and spring onion. Its firm sweet flesh and high price make it a celebration dish. Don't overpower it.
description: Chunky, mottled brown-and-cream fish with a broad head, large pectoral fins and a rounded tail — camouflaged like a river stone. Usually sold live.

iucnNote: Least Concern; more abundant and cheaper than the silver pomfret.
habitat: Coastal waters over muddy and sandy bottoms
flavour: Fuller and slightly oilier than silver pomfret
texture: Firm, moist flesh with relatively few bones
preparation: More robust than its silver cousin, so it takes well to frying and to curries as well as steaming. A good-value pomfret for assam or sweet-sour treatments.
description: Flat, oval body like the silver pomfret but darker — grey-brown to almost black — with a deeply forked tail. The pelvic fins are absent in adults.

iucnNote: Least Concern; valued mainly as the fish for springy hand-made fishballs.
texture: Firm, slightly sticky flesh prized for its bounce
preparation: The traditional fish for springy 'see dao' fishballs and fishcake — the flesh is scraped, salted and beaten until bouncy. Very bony to eat whole, so it's usually processed into paste.
description: Long, ribbon-like silvery body flattened side to side, with a single dorsal fin set far back and a pair of fang-like teeth. Bright and mirror-shiny.

iucnNote: Least Concern; an abundant, fast-breeding schooling fish and a sustainable, cheap, oily fish.
flavour: Rich and oily, full of omega-3
preparation: Grill or fry whole, or cook in spicy assam gravy. Rub with turmeric and salt first. Smaller ones are also salted and dried. A budget powerhouse of flavour.
description: Small, slender silvery-blue fish with a faint golden line and a single soft dorsal fin. Sold by the heap and very shiny.

iucnNote: Not formally evaluated as individual species, but anchovies are abundant, fast-breeding and short-lived — generally a low-impact choice.
sizeWeight: Sold by weight, fresh or dried
texture: Tiny and tender fresh; crisp when fried dried
preparation: The backbone of local cooking: dried ikan bilis is the base of nasi lemak sambal and stock. Fry until crisp and golden, or simmer for broth. Remove heads and guts of larger ones for a cleaner, less bitter taste.
description: Very small, slender silvery fish with a single bright silver stripe down the side. Most often seen dried in pale heaps.

iucnNote: Least Concern; an abundant reef-associated fish, also a major source of fish paste.
habitat: Sandy and muddy seabeds near reefs
preparation: A common everyday pink fish, fried whole until crisp or steamed. Much of the catch is also turned into fishball and surimi paste. Score the sides before frying.
description: Slender pink-to-rosy fish with a yellow-striped flank, large eyes and a forked tail whose upper lobe extends into a fine filament.

iucnNote: Least Concern; an abundant schooling fish and a sustainable budget pick.
texture: Firm-ish flesh, a touch coarser than ikan kuning
preparation: Fried whole until crisp and eaten with sambal, or grilled. Larger ones can be slit and stuffed with sambal before frying. Marinate with turmeric and salt.
description: Silvery scad with notably large eyes and a bony ridge of scutes along the rear of the lateral line. Bigger and rounder-eyed than ikan kuning.

iucnNote: Near Threatened, and other eels are worse — Japanese eel is Endangered and European eel Critically Endangered. Eels can't yet be bred in captivity at scale, so 'farmed' eel still starts from wild-caught young. Eat sparingly.
habitat: Rivers and estuaries; spawns far out at sea (catadromous)
preparation: Usually grilled with a sweet soy glaze (kabayaki/unagi) or braised. The skin is rich and gelatinous. Best filleted by a fishmonger — the blood and slime need careful cleaning.
description: Long, snake-like body with smooth slippery skin, a continuous fin running around the tail, and small pectoral fins. Olive-brown above, pale below.

iucnNote: Least Concern; a hardy air-breathing freshwater fish, widely and cheaply farmed.
habitat: Freshwater ponds, swamps and paddy fields
flavour: Mild, slightly earthy; rich when grilled
texture: Soft, moist and almost boneless along the fillet
preparation: Famous as ikan keli bakar — grilled over charcoal with sambal and a squeeze of calamansi. Also good in spicy assam gravy. Watch the sharp pectoral spines when handling.
description: Scaleless, slippery grey fish with long barbels (whiskers) around the mouth, a flattened head and a long low dorsal fin. Usually sold live.

iucnNote: Not formally evaluated. The world's most farmed prawn — the everyday, affordable prawn in most markets. Sourcing varies in sustainability.
habitat: Farmed in coastal and inland ponds
preparation: The budget all-rounder for noodles, fried rice, sambal and stir-fries. Sold fresh or frozen, often already deveined. Don't overcook — they turn mealy fast.
description: Pale translucent-grey prawn with whitish legs and a smooth, unbanded shell. Smaller and slimmer than tiger prawns.

iucnNote: Least Concern; widely farmed in freshwater ponds.
flavour: Very sweet, with rich orange head 'roe' (tomalley)
preparation: The star of big prawn noodle (har mee) and is wonderful steamed with garlic and glass noodles, or in white pepper. Squeeze the flavourful head juices into the broth. Mind the long claws.
description: Large freshwater prawn with very long, slender bluish claws (especially the second pair) and a greenish-blue body. The big orange head is full of flavour.

iucnNote: Data Deficient — fished but not formally assessed. Often sold locally as 'crayfish'.
preparation: All the meat is in the flat tail. Halve lengthwise and grill with garlic butter and cheese, or stir-fry with cereal or salted egg. Cooks quickly — just until the shell reddens and the meat sets.
description: Flat, shovel-shaped body with broad plate-like antennae at the front instead of long feelers, and no big claws. Sandy-brown and armoured.

iucnNote: Most tropical spiny lobster species are Data Deficient or unassessed and are slow-growing — a special-occasion buy.
preparation: A celebration seafood. Steam with garlic and glass noodles, or halve and grill with butter; the sweet tail and head fat are the prize. Cook live and don't overdo it — the meat turns tough.
description: Large lobster with very long spiny antennae, a heavily armoured spotted shell (often reddish-brown, green or speckled) and NO large front claws.

iucnNote: Not formally evaluated; widely farmed and a filter feeder. Mostly sold frozen or on the half-shell.
preparation: Steam on the half-shell with garlic and glass noodles, or sear hard and fast in a screaming-hot pan for a caramelised crust. Pat dry first. Overcooking turns them rubbery — seconds matter.
description: Round, fan-shaped fluted shell with the plump white adductor muscle (the 'scallop') and an orange roe. Often sold as the muscle alone or on a half-shell.

iucnNote: Treat as threatened: a 2022 global assessment found most commercially fished abalone species are Vulnerable, Endangered or Critically Endangered. Prefer farmed abalone, and check the species.
sizeWeight: Sold by piece; fresh, canned or dried
texture: Firm and chewy fresh; silky when braised long and slow
preparation: A prestige festive food. Fresh abalone is steamed briefly or sliced thin; dried abalone is braised for hours in superior stock. Score the surface in a crosshatch and don't overcook fresh ones or they toughen.
description: Single ear-shaped shell with a row of small breathing holes along one edge, iridescent inside; the muscular grey-brown foot is the edible part. Often sold canned or as the live animal.

iucnNote: Endangered — the prized sandfish has declined by over 90% across much of its range from overharvesting for the dried 'haisom' trade. A clear 'choose rarely' species; look for farmed sources.
flavour: Almost neutral — a carrier for rich braising sauces
preparation: A banquet ingredient (海参). Dried ones are rehydrated over days, then braised long and slow with mushrooms and superior stock until silky. Valued for texture rather than flavour.
description: Soft, sausage-shaped sea animal with a bumpy, leathery skin, sold mostly as the dark, hard, dried form that swells dramatically when soaked.

iucnNote: Not formally evaluated, but heavily collected; populations are under local pressure in parts of the region.
flavour: Sweet, briny and a touch chewy
preparation: A beloved zi char nibble: simply boiled, then the meat is pulled out with a toothpick and dipped in a sweet-sour chilli or hae ko (prawn paste) sauce. Twist gently as you pull so the meat doesn't break.
description: Small, heavy spiral conch shell with a flared, lipped opening, sandy-brown with darker mottling. Served in the shell, eaten with a toothpick.

iucnNote: Least Concern globally, but a high-value live-reef-fish-trade species under local pressure. Prefer farmed.
flavour: Sweet, clean and refined — a luxury steaming fish
preparation: A prized banquet fish, steamed Hong Kong style with light soy, ginger and spring onion. Its bright colour and sweet flesh command premium prices, especially live.
description: Vivid red-to-orange grouper covered in small bright-blue spots, with a rounded tail. Often sold live in tanks.

iucnNote: Vulnerable in the wild — slow-growing and heavily targeted. Most market supply is now farmed; choose farmed.
flavour: Sweet and rich with gelatinous skin
texture: Firm, moist, treasured for its silky collar and head
preparation: A classic steaming grouper for banquets; larger ones make a rich fish head curry. Steam whole with superior soy; the skin and head are the prized parts.
description: Heavy-bodied grouper with brown marbled blotches and dark saddles, a large mouth and small dark spots. Often sold live.

iucnNote: Least Concern; a prized large reef snapper.
preparation: Excellent steamed whole when small, or cut into steaks for curry when large. A reliable premium red fish.
description: Deep pink-red snapper, with three darker red bands across the body when young that fade with age. Classic snapper profile.

iucnNote: Least Concern; a common reef food fish.
preparation: Steam whole or grill. Good value with clean flesh; the cheeks and collar are tasty.
description: Olive-bronze emperor with blue spangles and wavy blue lines on the cheeks, a steep forehead and a forked tail.

iucnNote: Least Concern, but parrotfish are vital reef grazers — eat occasionally to keep reefs healthy.
preparation: Best steamed whole with ginger and soy, or fried. The flesh is delicate, so handle gently.
description: Stout fish with vivid blue-green-and-orange scales and a fused beak-like mouth (it scrapes algae off coral). Unmistakably colourful.

iucnNote: Least Concern; especially sought after around Chinese New Year for its prized roe.
flavour: Sweet, with creamy roe a festive delicacy
preparation: Pan-fry or steam whole; roe-laden fish (around CNY) are most prized. Mind the venomous fin spines when handling raw.
description: Oval silvery fish with fine spots, a small mouth and sharp venomous dorsal spines. Sold whole, often roe-heavy in season.

iucnNote: Least Concern; an abundant coastal predator.
habitat: Coastal and reef waters, a fast predator
preparation: Cut into steaks for curry, or fry. The firm flesh holds together well in spicy gravies. Often turned into fishcake.
description: Long, slender silver fish with a pointed head, prominent jaw and sharp teeth. Sold whole or as steaks.

iucnNote: Least Concern; a powerful, abundant trevally.
preparation: Larger fish are cut into thick steaks for curry or frying. Bleed and chill well after catching for the best flavour.
description: Deep, silvery, blunt-headed jack with a steep forehead and a strongly forked tail. Big and powerful-looking.

iucnNote: Least Concern; a common coastal predator, also salted and dried.
preparation: Often filleted and made into fishball or fishcake, or fried. The skin is tough, so it's usually skinned.
description: Long, compressed silvery fish with a row of dark blotches along the flank and a series of finlets near the tail.

iucnNote: Least Concern and increasingly farmed in sea cages — a fast-growing, fairly sustainable choice.
texture: Firm, dense — good for raw and grilling
preparation: Versatile: thick steaks for grilling or pan-searing, or sashimi when very fresh. Its firm flesh holds up to bold marinades.
description: Long, dark brown torpedo-shaped fish with a broad flat head and a horizontal stripe — superficially shark-like. Sold as loins and steaks.

iucnNote: Least Concern; very fast-growing and generally considered a sustainable choice.
preparation: Pan-fry, grill or curry. The lean flesh dries out easily, so cook it just to done and keep it moist with a sauce.
description: Striking fish with a blunt, high forehead (males), a long dorsal fin and brilliant green-gold-blue colours that fade after death. Sold as pinkish fillets.

iucnNote: Least Concern; widespread and farmed. Its salted, pressed roe is the prized 'wu yu zi' (bottarga).
habitat: Estuaries, coastal waters and brackish farms
preparation: Steam with lots of ginger to lift any muddiness, or pan-fry. The roe is salted and dried into a delicacy. Clean the gut cavity well.
description: Streamlined grey-silver fish with a blunt, flattened head, large scales and two short dorsal fins.

iucnNote: Least Concern and widely farmed for sashimi — a popular, fairly sustainable raw fish.
preparation: Best as sashimi or lightly seared when sashimi-grade. The fattier belly is prized. Also good grilled with teriyaki glaze.
description: Sold as pale pink, fatty loins with a silver skin and a faint yellow lateral stripe. Whole fish are sleek and torpedo-shaped.

iucnNote: Least Concern; an abundant small tuna, often the cheap 'tongkol' in markets.
preparation: Robust flavour suits spicy assam or sambal cooking. Often flaked for sambal goreng or made into fish floss. Bleed well; the dark flesh is strong.
description: Small tuna with a metallic blue back, broken oblique stripes and a few dark spots below the pectoral fin. Firm dark-red flesh.

iucnNote: Least Concern; the most abundant commercial tuna. Pole-and-line caught is the most sustainable.
preparation: Dried and smoked into katsuobushi (bonito flakes) for dashi; fresh, it's seared or curried. Strong flavour pairs well with bold spices.
description: Streamlined tuna with a dark blue back and 4–6 dark horizontal stripes on the silvery belly. Firm dark-red flesh.

iucnNote: Least Concern; the dried form ('ti poh') is the secret umami base of wonton noodle soup.
flavour: Mild, sweet; intensely savoury when dried
preparation: Fresh sole is pan-fried or steamed; the dried, toasted version is ground or fried crisp to flavour noodle broths. Fillets cleanly off the single flat bone.
description: Flat, tongue-shaped fish with both eyes on one (brown) side and a pale underside, tapering to a point. Sold fresh or dried flat.

iucnNote: Least Concern; a common, tasty reef-bottom fish.
habitat: Sandy and muddy areas near reefs
preparation: Pan-fry or steam whole. Despite small size, the flesh is sweet and well-regarded. Scale and gut, leave whole.
description: Reddish-pink fish with a pair of long whisker-like barbels under the chin used to probe the sand for food.

iucnNote: Least Concern; a good-eating reef fish.
preparation: Steam whole or cut into steaks. The thick, fleshy lips are considered a treat. Treat like a snapper.
description: Deep-bodied fish with notably thick, rubbery lips, often grey to dark with spots or stripes depending on age.

iucnNote: Least Concern; abundant schooling reef fish, a budget favourite.
preparation: Fry whole until crisp, or steam. An everyday cheap reef fish. Marinate with turmeric and salt before frying.
description: Spindle-shaped fish, often yellow-backed with a blue-silver belly and a deeply forked tail. Sold in colourful heaps.

iucnNote: Least Concern; an everyday inshore fish, much used for fishcake and otah.
texture: Soft, fine flesh — great for paste
preparation: Fried whole, in fish soup, or scraped into a smooth paste for otah and fishcake. Named for the croaking sound it makes.
description: Silvery-bronze fish with a blunt snout and a slightly arched back; some have a large swim bladder (the prized 'fish maw').

iucnNote: Least Concern; tiny, abundant and very cheap.
habitat: Coastal and estuarine waters in schools
preparation: Fry whole until crisp and eat bones and all with sambal, or simmer into a tasty broth. A humble, thrifty fish.
description: Small, flat, silvery fish with a slippery body and a protrusible mouth. Sold by the heap, very shiny.

iucnNote: Least Concern; a common coastal fish with (harmless) greenish bones.
preparation: Cut into sections and fry or curry. The bones turn green when cooked — that's natural and harmless. Often made into fishball.
description: Very long, thin fish with a slender body and an extended beak-like jaw full of needle teeth. Bright silver-green.

iucnNote: Least Concern; a common estuarine bream, also farmed.
preparation: A good steaming fish — Cantonese style with ginger and soy. Also nice grilled. Scale well; the skin crisps nicely.
description: Silvery, deep-bodied bream with yellowish pelvic and anal fins and a steep forehead. Looks like a classic 'sea bream'.

iucnNote: Least Concern; one of the common deep-red market snappers.
preparation: Steam whole for a premium dish, or use the head and collar for a rich curry. A reliable red fish for celebrations.
description: Deep crimson-red snapper, brighter on the back, with red fins and eyes. Larger and redder than the everyday mangrove red snapper.

iucnNote: Least Concern; another common red snapper of the wet market.
preparation: Steam whole or curry. Sometimes sold alongside malabar snapper as generic 'ikan merah'. Versatile and good value.
description: Bright pinkish-red snapper with large eyes and a classic snapper shape; juveniles have a dark saddle near the tail.

iucnNote: Data Deficient; a smaller relative of the batang, sold cheaper as steaks.
flavour: Rich and savoury, like a milder batang
preparation: Use just like batang — for sliced fish soup, curry, or pan-frying. A good-value alternative when batang is pricey.
description: Silvery torpedo-shaped mackerel covered in small dark spots (rather than bars). Sold as cross-section steaks.

iucnNote: Least Concern; an abundant reddish nocturnal fish, much used for surimi.
preparation: Fry or steam whole; much of the catch becomes fishball and surimi. Scale well — the scales are rough.
description: Bright red fish with enormous eyes and a large upturned mouth. Rough-scaled and unmistakably big-eyed.

iucnNote: Least Concern; abundant and a major raw material for fish paste and surimi.
flavour: Mild; valued for bounce, not flavour
preparation: Mostly scraped and beaten into springy fishball and fishcake paste, since it's bony to eat whole. Can also be fried crisp.
description: Cylindrical, lizard-like fish with a pointed head, big toothy mouth and mottled brownish body. Sold by the heap.

iucnNote: Many shark species are now Vulnerable, Endangered or Critically Endangered from overfishing and the fin trade — they breed slowly and are easily wiped out. Best avoided; if buying, know the species and never buy shark fin.
flavour: Mild, slightly ammoniated if not bled
preparation: Sometimes sold as cheap boneless steaks for curry. Soak in milk or acidulated water to remove any ammonia smell. We'd encourage choosing a sustainable fish instead.
description: Sold as pale, boneless steaks with a central cartilage core and rough skin. Small whole sharks have the classic torpedo shape and dorsal fin.

iucnNote: Assessed Vulnerable — heavily trawled across the region. Choose the more abundant pink threadfin bream where possible.
preparation: Fried whole or steamed; also a major fish-paste species. Score the sides before frying for crisp skin.
description: Slender pink-and-yellow fish with bright yellow stripes along the flank and a long yellow filament on the upper tail lobe.

iucnNote: Least Concern; a common flatfish of muddy coasts.
preparation: Pan-fry whole until crisp, or steam gently. The flesh is delicate; lift the fillets cleanly off the flat frame.
description: Flat, oval fish with both eyes on the upper (mottled brown) side and a white underside, lying flush against the seabed.

iucnNote: Least Concern; abundant and cheap, famous stuffed with sambal.
preparation: The classic 'sambal-stuffed cencaru' — slit the side, pack in sambal and pan-fry. The hard scutes near the tail are trimmed off.
description: Streamlined scad with a row of very hard bony scutes (the 'hardtail') along the rear flank and a deeply forked tail.

iucnNote: Least Concern; a hugely abundant schooling baitfish and budget food fish.
habitat: Coastal pelagic waters in large schools
preparation: Fried, grilled or simmered in spicy gravy. Also salted and dried. Rub with turmeric and salt before frying.
description: Slim, rounded silvery scad with a single small finlet behind the dorsal and anal fins and a narrow tail base.

iucnNote: Least Concern; a fast-growing oceanic mackerel.
preparation: Excellent grilled or pan-seared as steaks; also great in sliced fish soup and curry. Cook just to done — the lean flesh dries out.
description: Long, slender steel-blue fish with faint vertical bars and a pointed snout. Sold as firm white steaks and loins.

iucnNote: Least Concern; common in muddy estuaries, a thrifty fish.
texture: Soft, moist, boneless along the fillet
preparation: Good in spicy assam gravy or grilled. Watch the sharp, mildly venomous pectoral and dorsal spines when handling.
description: Scaleless grey fish with barbels around the mouth and a forked tail (unlike the freshwater keli). Sharp leading fin spines.

iucnNote: Least Concern; a hardy estuarine fish, also kept as an aquarium fish.
preparation: Steam or fry whole. Mind the venomous dorsal spines. The deep body yields a good amount of flesh for its size.
description: Round, deep-bodied silver-green fish covered in large dark spots ('money fish'), with a small mouth and spiny fins.

iucnNote: Least Concern; imported live from the North Atlantic. A well-managed fishery overall.
texture: Firm, dense claw and tail meat
preparation: Unlike spiny lobster, it has big meaty claws. Steam or boil, or halve and bake with garlic butter and cheese. Cook live; don't overcook or the meat toughens.
description: Large lobster with two big front claws (one heavy crusher, one slender), a long segmented tail and long antennae. Dark greenish-blue alive, red when cooked.

iucnNote: Not formally evaluated; farmed in freshwater ponds. (Different from the 'crayfish' name locals give slipper lobster.)
flavour: Sweet, mild, between prawn and lobster
preparation: Stir-fry in mala, cereal or salted-egg sauce, or boil and eat with the hands. Purge in clean water first. The claws hold a little sweet meat too.
description: Small lobster-like crustacean with a pair of claws (males have a red patch on the claw) and a segmented tail. Bluish to brownish-green.

iucnNote: Not formally evaluated; an unusual crab, less common but tasty.
texture: Tender, a touch firmer than flower crab
preparation: Steam, or stir-fry with black pepper or ginger and spring onion. The body meat is sweet; the legs are slimmer than mud crab.
description: Orange-red crab with an elongated, frog-like body, paddle-tipped legs that fold forward, and small pincers. Distinctive narrow shape.

iucnNote: Not formally evaluated; a common small wild and farmed prawn, prized for sweetness.
habitat: Estuaries and coastal waters with muddy bottoms
flavour: Intensely sweet — many say sweeter than big prawns
preparation: The hawker favourite for white pepper prawns, har cheong gai marinades and prawn noodles. The harder shell crisps beautifully when fried — eat shell and all.
description: Small prawn with a noticeably hard, slightly hairy shell and a greenish-grey, speckled colour. Sold by the kati.

iucnNote: Not formally evaluated; tiny and abundant. The raw material for belacan, cincalok and hae bee.
sizeWeight: Sold by the kg (fresh or fermented)
habitat: Coastal surface waters in huge swarms
texture: Tiny and soft; gritty if not cleaned
preparation: Rarely eaten whole — it is fermented into belacan (shrimp paste) and cincalok, or dried into hae bee (dried shrimp). The flavour backbone of countless local dishes.
description: Minuscule, almost transparent shrimp with long whiskers, sold as a pale pinkish mass — fresh, salted, or as the brown fermented paste.

iucnNote: Data Deficient; a fast-growing, short-lived squid and a major fishery species.
texture: Tender if cooked fast, rubbery if overdone
preparation: The everyday market squid for sambal sotong, sotong kang kong and stir-fries. Pull out the clear quill, score the body, and cook hot and fast.
description: Slim, torpedo-shaped translucent-pink squid with a pointed mantle, narrow triangular fins at the tail end, and ten arms.

iucnNote: Data Deficient; small octopus species, often sold frozen and cleaned.
texture: Tender when small; quick to toughen
preparation: Great in spicy stir-fries, takoyaki, salads or skewers. Small ones don't need the long tenderising big octopus does — blanch briefly then sauce. Clean off the beak and ink.
description: Small octopus with a soft round head and eight short suckered arms, sold whole and cleaned, often frozen in blocks.

iucnNote: Data Deficient; a large reef cuttlefish, prized for its thick meat.
preparation: Its thick mantle is excellent scored and stir-fried, blanched for Teochew cold cuttlefish, or grilled. Remove the cuttlebone and ink sac (keep the ink for black noodle dishes).
description: Large, broad cuttlefish with a wide fringing fin around the body and a thick internal cuttlebone. Mottled, able to flash colours.

iucnNote: Not formally evaluated; commonly sold frozen and blanched, often for sushi.
preparation: The orange-tipped 'tongue' is blanched and used in sushi (hokkigai) and salads; whole clams are stir-fried or added to hotpot. Cooks in seconds.
description: Smooth, triangular off-white shell; the edible foot is pale with a distinctive orange-red tip when blanched.

iucnNote: Not formally evaluated; widely farmed on mudflats and very common in markets.
preparation: Purge in salted water, then stir-fry with sambal or steam with garlic and wine. A workhorse clam for soups and stir-fries. Discard any that don't open.
description: Smooth, glossy, thick triangular shell with concentric growth lines and pretty tan-to-grey markings; heavier than the carpet clam.

iucnNote: Not formally evaluated; a long-lived clam, imported live as a sashimi luxury.
texture: Crunchy and crisp — the prized part is the siphon
preparation: Blanch the siphon briefly, peel off the dark skin, then slice thin for sashimi or a quick hotpot swish. The body meat is good stir-fried. Overcooking ruins the signature crunch.
description: Huge clam with a long, protruding siphon ('trunk') that can't retract into the gaping shell. Sold live, the siphon drooping over the shell.

iucnNote: Least Concern; rope-farmed and imported (often from NZ/Europe). A low-impact, sustainable protein.
habitat: Farmed on ropes in cool coastal waters
flavour: Briny, savoury, a touch richer than green mussel
preparation: Scrub and debeard. Steam open in white wine or a tomato-chilli broth, or grill on the half shell. Discard any that won't open after cooking. Ready in ~5 minutes.
description: Smooth, glossy blue-black to purple-brown teardrop shell, often sold live in mesh bags or as frozen half-shells.

iucnNote: Not formally evaluated; hand-gathered from mangroves, a nostalgic kampung snack.
preparation: Stir-fry hard with sambal, lemongrass and tamarind, then suck the spicy meat out of the open end (the tip is snipped off so you can slurp). Soak well to purge mud first.
description: Small, pointed, dark spiral snail shells gathered from the mangroves; served in a fragrant sambal gravy, eaten by slurping.

iucnNote: Not formally evaluated; a larger sea snail, hand-collected from rocky reefs.
preparation: Boil with aromatics, then twist the meat out and slice; serve with chilli dip, or stir-fry with sambal. Trim off the dark gut coil before eating.
description: Heavy, rounded spiral shell with a pearly interior and a hard 'operculum' (the cat's-eye trapdoor). Sold live in the shell.

iucnNote: Not formally evaluated; small surf-zone clams raked from sandy beaches.
preparation: Purge thoroughly (they hold sand), then stir-fry hard with sambal or steam quickly with garlic. Lots of little shells make a great sharing dish. Discard unopened ones.
description: Small, smooth, wedge-shaped shells in pretty pastel bands of cream, pink, purple and brown. Sold live in heaps.

iucnNote: Not formally evaluated; increasingly farmed, popular in hotpot and stir-fries.
flavour: Sweet and clean, less chewy than conch
preparation: Boil briefly and dip in chilli, blanch in hotpot, or stir-fry with sambal or XO sauce. Twist the meat out and remove the dark gut tip. Quick to overcook.
description: Smooth, glossy cream-coloured spiral shell patterned with squarish brown-orange blotches. Sold live in the shell.

iucnNote: Not formally evaluated; large mangrove clams gathered from the mud, a kampung favourite.
preparation: The big meaty clam for masak lemak and curries — its bold flavour stands up to coconut and spice. Soak well to purge mud; scrub the heavy shells. Cook through fully.
description: Thick, heavy, rounded dark-brown to blackish shell with concentric ridges, encrusted with mud when fresh. Large and chunky.

iucnNote: Not formally evaluated, but reef urchins can be quickly overharvested locally. A delicacy to enjoy occasionally.
sizeWeight: Edible roe is a few grams per urchin
preparation: Only the orange-gold roe (uni) inside is eaten — usually raw on sushi or over rice, sometimes in pasta or steamed egg. Crack open the shell carefully and lift out the lobes.
description: Round, spiny shell (test) covered in short spines; inside are five soft orange-gold tongues of roe — the prized part.

iucnNote: Not formally evaluated; harvested and processed in brine. Generally an abundant, fast-reproducing resource.
flavour: Almost neutral, lightly briny — carries dressing
preparation: Bought salted; rinse well and soak to remove salt, then blanch briefly and shock in ice water to keep the crunch. Dress with sesame oil, vinegar and soy for a cold appetiser.
description: Sold as pale, crinkly, semi-translucent shredded strips (the processed, salted bell of an edible jellyfish), not as the whole animal.
